name: The Tideborn
description:
They came from across the sea. Not as conquerors — as readers.

The Tideborn built ships that crossed the open ocean not through brute engineering but through a navigational art that treated the sea as a living text. They read the currents the way scholars read books — with fluency earned over generations. Their ships are grown, not built — living wood shaped over decades by Bond-attuned shipwrights who coax the timber into forms that flex with the waves instead of fighting them.

What brought them here wasn't trade or territory. Their gods told them to come. Not through prophecy or command — through a feeling, shared simultaneously across every Tideborn priest, that something was happening across the water. A disturbance in the divine ecology. New gods being born. Old patterns breaking. The Tideborn don't ignore when their gods speak, because their gods don't speak often, and when they do, it matters.

The Tideborn relationship with divinity is what shocks the settled world most. Where Thornwall fears its gods and Solmare studies them and the Rootwalkers sing to them, the Tideborn NEGOTIATE with theirs. Their gods have names, personalities, preferences, and limits — and the Tideborn know all of them, because they've been in deliberate, reciprocal relationship with their pantheon for longer than any culture on this continent has existed.

They don't worship. They partner. The distinction is everything.

A Tideborn priest doesn't pray for rain. A Tideborn priest asks the rain-speaker what it would cost, what it would need, and whether now is a good time. Sometimes the answer is no. The Tideborn accept no, because they understand that a god who always says yes is a god who isn't listening.

What the settled world has that horrifies them: walls. Armies. Kings who rule through fear-worship. Gods that are fed dread instead of dialogue. The Tideborn see a continent of people who've imprisoned themselves and their gods in a system of coercion and called it civilization.

What they don't understand: scale. The Tideborn have never needed centralized authority because their communities are small, mobile, and connected through the gods themselves. They've never needed standing armies because their gods protect them — willingly, as partners. They've never needed written law because their oral traditions are maintained with divine assistance, each story verified by the god who witnessed it.

None of this works at the scale of a continent. The Tideborn are about to learn why walls exist.
knowledge_system: Oral-divine. Knowledge is stored in partnership with gods who act as living archives. A Tideborn elder can ask their patron god to recall a conversation from three generations ago, verbatim. This is more reliable than any library — and completely non-transferable. If the god dies or the relationship breaks, the knowledge is gone forever.
historical_inspiration: Polynesian navigators (ocean-reading, wayfinding without instruments) + Haudenosaunee Confederacy (sophisticated governance without centralized state) + Aboriginal Australian songlines (knowledge encoded in landscape relationship) — all flipped into the arriving civilization rather than the contacted one. The question: what happens when a sophisticated-in-different-dimensions civilization meets a scaled-but-spiritually-impoverished one?
